posts.caption:
5,783 contact sheets spanning Peter Hujar’s photographic life are stored in eight banker boxes. Each sheet is encased in a plastic wallet, separated, and sorted vertically like documents in a filing cabinet. ⁠
⁠
There is nothing especially remarkable about these prosaic boxes: colourless, functional, quietly banal. They could easily be passed over without a second glance. Yet inside, they hold an entire world. On the occasion of Hujar: Contact, a new book published by @mack_publishing alongside an exhibition of the same name at @themorganlibrary and Museum, curator Joel Smith and archivist @olivia.mc speak to both the artistic and personal intimacy of Hujar’s contact sheets.⁠
⁠
“Nothing that supersedes the intimacy of the contact sheets themselves,” McCall tells @w.lliam. Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Hujar: Contact. Courtesy of MACK; Fraenkel Gallery, San Francisco; and Ortuzar, New York. © The Peter Hujar Archive / Artists Rights Society (ARS)⁠
1. Peter Hujar, contact prints, David Wojnarowicz, 1981, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
2. Peter Hujar, contact prints, Susan Sontag at home, 1975, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
3. Peter Hujar, contact prints, Jackie Curtis at 10 East 23rd Street, 1970, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
5. Peter Hujar, contact prints, self-portraits at 189 Second Avenue, 1974, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
6. Portraits of Hujar from 1957, 1959, 1960, 1971, and 1976, contact prints, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
7. Peter Hujar photographed by Arielle Dombasle, contact print, 189 Second Avenue, 1983, from Hujar: Contact by Joel Smith (MACK and The Morgan Library & Museum, 2026)⁠
⁠
@peterhujararchive #PeterHujar⁠

posts.caption:
Cindy Crawford poses in front of a hollowed tree in St Tropez, like Botticelli’s Venus; Hiro trains Maria Beadeux to smoke, inspired by the opium dens that he saw as a child growing up in the 1930s; William Wegman’s 1981 triptych of a dog quietly sat by a pond. Each work currently on display at @hamiltonsgallery as part of its new exhibition, Up Close, tests the distance between subject and lens. ⁠
⁠
Opening in tandem with Photo London and running for the month of May, Up Close is a survey of intimacy, detail and closeness. “With Photo London on the calendar, we began thinking about how the fair encourages people to get up close to photography,” Tim Jefferies, Hamiltons gallery founder and principal tells @rose_dodd. ⁠
⁠
Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Up Close at Hamiltons⁠
1. Hiro, Maria Beadeux, New York, 1974 © Estate of Hiro Wakabayashi. Courtesy of Hamiltons Gallery⁠
2. Herb Ritts, Naomi Seated, Hollywood, 1991 © Herb Ritts Foundation, Courtesy of Hamiltons Gallery⁠
3. Woman watching rocket blast off through open window, Paris, 1968 © Helmut Newton Foundation, Courtesy of Hamiltons Gallery⁠
4. Helmut Newton, Cindy on the Place de Lys, St Tropez, for American Vogue, 1991 © Helmut Newton Foundation, Courtesy of Hamiltons Gallery⁠
5-6. Daidō Moriyama, Tokyo Ringway - Route 16 (from “On the Road”), 1969 © Daidō Moriyama, Courtesy of Hamiltons Gallery

posts.caption:
Before The L Word, there was KUTT: the short-lived sister zine to iconoclastic gay magazine BUTT. Lasting for just three issues between 2002 and 2003, the publication took BUTT’s signature pink pages and tongue-in-cheek attitude and applied them to a survey of the lesbian scene in Amsterdam and beyond.⁠
⁠
Over two decades on from its original run, copies of the magazine are scarce and in increasingly high demand. Responding to this reignited enthusiasm for the project, the contents of each zine have now been collated in a new book published by @idea.ltd, set to unite this cult title with a fresh generation of readers.⁠
⁠
“I think younger people are interested in printed matter, it’s nice to make something printed in amongst this digital overload of images [on social media],” former editor @jessicagysel tells @meganhwallace. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Courtesy of KUTT and IDEA⁠
1. Vol 1. Chloe Sevigny by Martien Mulder, 2002⁠
2. Cover⁠
3-4. Vol 3. Emmeline and Marlous by Viviane Sassen, 2003⁠
6. Vol 2. South of No North by Collier Schorr, 1995⁠
7-8. Vol 1. The Joy of Lesbian Sex, 2002⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
When Andrew Bolton first reached out to @sjodwyer and @karolinevitto about the @metmuseum’s new Costume Institute exhibition, both designers expected to be meeting with an assistant. But each ended up on long, searching calls with Bolton himself – a detail that underpins the seriousness with which Costume Art, the inaugural show in the Costume Institute’s newly expanded galleries, was conceived. ⁠
⁠
Pairing garments with works from across The Met’s collection – etchings, sculpture, painting – the exhibition presents the dressed body as a subject worthy of the same scrutiny as any other art historical question. For O’Dwyer and Vitto, two of the most significant independent designers working with extended sizing in fashion today, being included felt both overdue and, given the current climate, strangely timely. ⁠
⁠
In an exclusive conversation with @mad.e.leiner, the two designers discuss what it means to have this work archived, the words we still haven’t got right, and the “slow-motion car crash” of thinning they’ve both been watching for years. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 1. Courtesy of the designers⁠
3. Karoline Vitto Autumn/Winter 2026. Courtesy of Karoline Vitto⁠
5. Courtesy of Metropolitan Museum of Art⁠
7, 9. Sinéad O’Dwyer Autumn/Winter 2025 campaign. Courtesy of Sinéad O’Dwyer

WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.

posts.caption:
On 26 May at 7pm, Bowie Nights: Behind The Lens took place at @_lightroomldn. Lightroom is currently hosting an immersive David Bowie exhibition, and this special event brought together six photographers who captured some of his most iconic images (@geoffmaccormackcollection, @richardyounggallery, @thedenisoregan, @kcmanc, @tonymcgeeofficial, and @chrisduffyphoto). Hosted by journalist and author @msmirandasawyer, each photographer spent six minutes discussing three large-scale Bowie images on display, sharing the stories behind them. Even ten years after Bowie’s passing, he continues to be remembered and celebrated in their own way. Swipe right to see Bowie as captured by these six photographers.
Words by Julie Jeong

posts.caption:
Pierpaolo Piccioli’s Balenciaga opened a new IG account, @Keeppprolling. Commonly known as a “finsta” — short for fake Instagram. @Keeppprolling is neither the brand’s official account nor Pierpaolo Piccioli’s personal account, but a new space that exists somewhere in between.
A Finsta means a second account created by Gen Z to show their “Real me” only to a small group of close friends. Unlike a carefully curated main feed, finsta is filled with raw emotions, contextless photos, and half-formed thoughts, which is a place where imperfection is allowed. What’s interesting is that this mindset is now making its way into fashion houses as well.
@Keeppprolling follows only three accounts: actress Sarah Pidgeon, filmmaker Celine Song, and Pierpaolo Piccioli. The official Balenciaga account is not included in its following list. It’s only in the profile bio. This small choice alone defines the character of the account: cinema over fashion, people over brand.
Even the name itself is telling: @Keeppprolling. A phrase from film sets, the director’s voice saying keep the camera rolling, don’t stop shooting. Of course, what this account is for remains unclear. It could be a space for content that doesn’t fit strict brand guidelines, a strategy to build a more flexible and human connection with the audience, or simply Piccioli doing something he finds interesting. Either way, it speaks directly to the sensibility of this moment.
The reason people create finstas ultimately comes down to one thing: narrowing the gap between the “self” they want to present and the “self” that simply exists. Balenciaga also seems to be exploring the gap right now. Where this new finsta will lead is still unknown. But for now, the camera is rolling. Keep Rolling, Keep Piccioli, Keep Balenciaga!
+ As of 27 May, @Keeppprolling has 2,302 followers. How many will it gain?
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @keeppprolling
#Balenciaga #PierpaoloPiccioli #Keeppprolling #SarahPidgeon #CelineSong @balenciaga @pppiccioli @helloellephanta @sarah__pidgeon

posts.caption:
BTS returned to the American Music Awards for the first time in five years to accept Artist of the Year wearing distressed leather outerwear by Los Angeles label @warpaint.xyz
The brand’s cracked leather textures, raw hems, washed military tones, and oversized silhouettes brought a darker, underground edge to the AMAs stage closer to early 2000s avant-garde menswear than conventional award-show styling.
WarPaint later confirmed @rkive and @agustd of @bts.bighitofficial wore the pieces while announcing an upcoming Los Angeles pop-up and June online drop!

posts.caption:
Jean Paul Gaultier’s Fall 2004 collection, “Beauty of Disguise,” transformed the runway into something closer to surrealist theater.
The show’s most unforgettable element was the masks: full-head coverings made from pale stretch fabric, printed with surrealist faces and fitted loosely to each model’s head. Some featured exaggerated makeup prints, while others appeared almost blank. Disguising the models into anonymous mannequins suspended somewhere between human and artificial.
More than an accessory, the masks reflected one of Gaultier’s longest-running fascinations: disguise, transformation, and the idea that identity itself can be constructed through clothing.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)

They highlight design as cultural storytelling and the responsibility that comes with shaping how the world experiences “local” in a global age.
By invitation only.

With the new summer resort collection inspired by jet-set destinations like Ibiza, The Hamptons, Cannes and Capri, Louis Vuitton (@louisvuitton) brings forward vacation-ready pieces and breezy fabrics that capture effortless elegance, all under the artistic direction of Nicolas Ghesquière (@nicolasghesquiere).

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‘H2O: Just Add Water,’ and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director’s chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‘The Dark Lake’. While she’s relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.“I feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you’re told, ‘This is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors’. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.”
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that “lean into the complexities of being a woman.”

posts.caption:
“I’m attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,” @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‘Two Years Later’, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”
Continue reading Phoebe Tonkin’s cover story through the link in bio.

posts.caption:
A cluster of independent fine jewellery brands are drawing on favourable market conditions and evolving shopper preferences to move beyond their original niches and compete with established mega brands like Cartier and Tiffany & Co. for some of the world’s most prestigious clients.
Growth in the fragmented market for branded jewellery has prompted increased competition among Place Vendôme giants as the category becomes a key priority for luxury groups.
Rachel Garrahan (@rgarrahan) explores how independent jewellers are cultivating lifelong collectors through personal relationships, distinctive design and immersive retail spaces. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @jessica_mccormack, @nikoskoulisjewels
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 26, 2026. May be an image of magazine, mannequin, sewing machine and text that says 'BOF 23 2 33 35 How Ozempic Is Forcing Fashion to Rethink Fit It's not just about ordering more smalls. Brands must grapple with growing 'size volatility' as more consumers use GLP-1 drugs.'.

posts.caption:
The rise of weight-loss drugs is exposing and accelerating a problem fashion has long struggled to solve: sizing systems built for static bodies in a world where people’s bodies are anything but, experts say.
GLP-1 users may have an appetite for shopping, but many of their individual needs can vary significantly. Headlines focused on people simply getting thinner oversimplify reality: widespread GLP-1 use is reshaping bodies in more complex ways.
For fashion firms navigating a broader slowdown — buoyed by inflation, geopolitical uncertainty and softening demand — there’s a clear opportunity. But it’s a complicated one to unlock.
BoF's Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) explores fashion’s new sizing challenge in the age of GLP-1s. Read more #linkinbio
📷 Unsplash

posts.caption:
In 2026, the luxury fashion industry has been placing bets, disproportionately and deliberately, on women athletes.
The relationship between elite female athletes and the fashion and beauty industries is not new: Zheng Qinwen is a Dior ambassador; Iga Swiatek carries Lancôme. But the architecture of these relationships is changing fast, and in one direction only.
The question becomes: what are those athletes being offered in return?
Olivier Rivard-Cohen argues the framework that turns visibility into brand ownership for top female athletes is almost entirely unbuilt. Read his full opinion piece at the #linkinbio
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on May 26, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says '"The debate over whether women's sport generates value has been settled- by the media rights, the fashion houses competing for Met Gala tie-ups, the beauty brands naming arenas. What has not been settled is structural: the layer that transforms a brand deal into an equity position, a career into an institution." OLIVIER RIVARD- RIVARD-COHEN -COHEN STRATEGIC ADVISOR BOF'.

posts.caption: Mined diamonds aren’t all that rare. It’s easy to research how De Beers created this false scarcity through clever marketing and hoarding. The issue is two fold now though: African folks are taking back control of their own resources just as lab diamonds take over and disrupt the whole industry. Coincidence? It’s not an easy choice to make - for me, lab grown was the obvious choice at the time that my fiance and I were discussing getting engaged because I had no idea that supply chains could be more traceable and that a long legacy of colonial mine control was coming to an end (and I wanted a larger rock too - nothing gaudy though hehe). I think it comes down to personal choice and taste and everyone needs to make the choice that’s right for them. My engagement ring is lab grown while my inherited jewelry from my grandmother obviously contains natural diamonds. I love both ❤️

posts.caption:
This week, Chanel reported its annual results for 2025. Revenue rose 2 percent to $19.3 billion, defying a luxury downturn.
But the number that caught the industry’s attention wasn’t the top line — it was the acceleration. In the second half of the year, Chanel’s sales grew by high single digits across every category and every region — and this was before designs by the house’s new artistic director, Matthieu Blazy, had even arrived in stores. The excitement alone was enough to change the trajectory.
The momentum has two sources. One is Blazy, whose runway debut last October has sparked what the industry is calling “Blazymania”, a level of excitement Chanel hasn’t seen in years. The other is the leadership of chief executive Leena Nair, who has spent her tenure investing heavily in Chanel’s retail network, its manufacturing, and its people — building the foundations that allowed a creative spark to catch fire.
Two years before they would work together at Chanel, Blazy and Nair spoke at #BoFVOICES 2023. Separately they each made the case for craft, people and long-term thinking over speed and scale.
This week on The BoF Podcast, we revisit their talks to understand how their synergistic philosophies are powering Chanel’s newfound momentum.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #BoFPodcast
📷 Getty Images

posts.caption:
Hi this is @ImranAmed, welcome to my weekly letter.
On the surface, there was plenty to celebrate at the Financial Times’ Business of Luxury Summit this week. Delphine Arnault and Jonathan Anderson marked one year of their joint effort to re-energise Dior. Stéphane de La Faverie, chief executive of The Estée Lauder Companies, underscored the beauty conglomerate’s nine consecutive months of growth after four years of decline. And Victoria Beckham said both her fashion and beauty divisions are now profitable after almost two decades of losses.
But beneath the public relations spin, a reckoning is underway as the luxury industry struggles to recover from a punishing downturn in demand.
When asked what is the greatest risk facing the luxury industry, Bain’s Claudia D’Arpizio didn’t cite tariffs, the war in the Middle East or slow growth in China, but rising inequality: the risk that luxury goods are becoming markers of exclusion rather than totems of aspiration.
This is a dynamic The Business of Fashion has been tracking for at least a decade. That risk has only increased amid the rise of America’s tech centibillionaires, Asia’s family empires and the global hedge fund class. Contrary to popular belief, “old money” doesn’t drive the luxury sector: The majority of luxury growth comes from aspirants and the upwardly mobile — you get a better life and you mark the occasion and signal your newfound status by buying something beautiful. But when wealth polarisation becomes so extreme that the ladder itself feels broken, aspiration melts away. People stop admiring the wealthy, and start resenting them.
According to research by Bain, the luxury market lost 50 million customers between 2022 and 2024, shrinking from 400 million to 350 million globally, amid macroeconomic gloom and extravagant price increases. “Very important customers” — the top 2 percent of spenders — now account for 45 percent of all luxury purchases, up from 35 percent in 2021, while millions of shoppers have walked away.
So, how did we end up here? Read my full letter at the #linkinbio

posts.caption:
one of my truly fav collections of all time - Christopher John Rogers Fall 2020 💛 The whole collection is a swarm of colors with each more exciting than the previous one. Trash bags was one of the inspirations of this glorious collection along with Madame Grès, pink and orange carryout bags, and monochromatic church wear. The set design by Sauras + Garriga was old-school glam meets cozy comfort, an absolute beauty.
CJR’s extravagant silhouettes included exaggerated sleeves, cinched waists, dramatic skirts, big shoulders, and ruffled collars. The colors in this collection, Magnificent. Orange, yellow, olive, neon, violet, fuchsia, red, maroons, pink; all of them on acid times a hundred. We saw promising tailoring in some gleaming suits and some intense monochromatic color-blocked suits in shades of yellow and red. Each look here was better than the next, among these came a big blazing brilliant teal gown.This collection will forever have a place in my heart, something about it connects very deeply within.
It reminded me how powerful fashion can feel when it fully commits to joy, color, and drama without looking forced. everything felt alive, from the silhouettes to the styling. it had that rare energy where you just enjoy the clothes for what they are.

posts.caption:
When Icelandic Chinese musician Laufey dropped her latest music video last month, starring a cast of mixed white and Asian celebrities, the internet went wild.
"Laufey collecting wasians like pokemon," read one social media comment, using a portmanteau to describe people of mixed white and Asian descent. "WASIAN AVENGERS," another comment cheered.
She is among those who have publicly embraced the Wasian label — which has helped many people who self-identify as such to feel more seen.
This heightened attention coincides with a growing fascination in the West around Asian entertainment and culture (think the Netflix smash hit "KPop Demon Hunters," the recent Chinamaxxing trend, K-beauty products, and even Labubus). Increased visibility has crossed over into the real world, with "Wasian meetups" taking place earlier this May in San Francisco and New York City.
For some, it was a powerful and positive experience to see themselves represented and to connect with others.
But the meetups have also triggered a heated debate about race and identity that has reached a fever pitch online in recent weeks. The controversy has raised questions about who is represented, and why — as well as what it means to be multiracial in America.
Tap the link in @cnn’s bio for more.
📸 Nicole Mago/Warren Fu/Quentin Nguyen-duy
📹 Quentin Nguyen-duy

posts.caption:
When she was four, the artist Widline Cadet (@widline_ ) was separated from her mother for six years as she emigrated from Haiti to New York to pursue a better life for her family.
At 10 years old, Cadet reunited with her mother in New York, but as she grew into adulthood, she realized that she didn't know her well at all. Nor did Cadet have a larger sense of her family, the ancestral threads that weave back through time.
Photographs of her own childhood and family were scarce. Her mother didn't have a picture of her own mother. Memories faded with each passing year.
For nearly a decade, Cadet has been crafting her own "living archive," exploring the diasporic experience and the elusiveness of memory. The largest presentation of her work to date is on view at @milwaukeeart.
📸: Widline Cadet

In @bootsriley’s @iloveboostersmovie, color serves as its own character, fueling the full-throttle chaos of his latest eccentric comedy about a group of girlfriends who steal and resell—or "boost"—clothes to make ends meet.
Starring @keke, @naomiackie, @taylour – who play Corvette, Sade and Mariah in the Velvet Gang — and @demimoore, who serves as the captivating retail mogul-villain Christie Smith, Riley's sophomore feature is a raucous critique of over-consumption and, at times, a sobering attack on the fashion industry's ethical failings.
The film's costumes – which evoke a Bay-area-grunge-meets-Gen-Z-raver type of vibe – are the colorful concoction of Oscar-nominated costume designer @shirleykurata.
At the link in bio, read how Kurata worked alongside Riley to craft the wild, neon-drenched world of 'Boosters'.
📸: NEON

posts.caption:
At the Frick Museum on Wednesday night, @louisvuitton introduced François Boucher’s putti to Keith Haring’s radiant babies.
For the label’s Cruise 2027 collection, staged in the hallowed halls of the @frickcollection a New York City Gilded Age mansion-turned-art gallery, designer @nicolasghesquiere created several pieces adorned with works by the late US pop artist Keith Haring.
Haring’s trademark faceless figures dominated satin tops, dresses, bags, leather jackets and high-top sneakers. “Pop art, pop culture and pop luxury,” read the brand’s accompanying press release. “The notion of popular as a powerful medium, the conveyance of messages to all.”
🎥: CNN
📸 : Louis Vuitton

posts.caption:
What does it take to build a cult-favorite fashion brand as a teenager—without a design background, without investors, and without anyone fully taking you seriously? For sisters @brendaliang and @clarahychi, founders of @sirenbasics, the answer was an unshakeable belief in what they were building.
Born from a simple desire for playful, beautiful underwear that didn’t demand a luxury price tag, Siren Basics has grown into one of this generation’s most beloved intimates brands (co-signed by the likes of @britneyspears, @devonleecarlson, and @treaclychild). We sat down with the duo to talk about the “unsexy” reality of building a business and why underwear deserves to be taken seriously at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Honestly, I don’t understand why it’s almost always the same girls, wearing the same outfits, the white t-shirt, the thong sandals, the same minimalist aesthetic, who automatically get to dictate what’s considered fashionable.
And I don’t mean this as criticism toward people who dress that way.
What surprises me is that, despite the incredible diversity of bodies, styles, and genuinely original, creative, and far more daring fashion ideas out there, the conversation still seems to revolve around the same aesthetic formula.
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by COVETEUR on February 10, 2026 tagging @cscotttt, @faith_xue, @cgonzalezbeauty, @ella_okeeffe, @tashasheridan, @blakeerik, @dionemdavis, @harnwaw, @quillemons, @carrieashleyh, @kalaherh, @otherhalfcreative, and @luxpascal_.

posts.caption:
DSquared2 SS2010 was pure excess in the best possible way. Dean and Dan Caten built the show around hyper-sexual glamour, distressed denim, tiny shorts, low-rise silhouettes, oversized belts, body-hugging dresses and chaotic layering that felt somewhere between backstage rockstar culture and early 2000s paparazzi fashion. Nothing about the collection was subtle. Every look pushed this very unapologetic idea of confidence, sex appeal and nightlife energy that defined so much of fashion during that era.
What made the collection stand out was how aggressively styled everything felt. The ripped denim, heavy accessories, bronzed skin, towering heels and exaggerated silhouettes created characters rather than just outfits. DSquared2 understood fashion as spectacle and attitude first, which is probably why these runway shows still feel memorable years later.
Looking back now, the collection feels surprisingly relevant again. Fashion has slowly moved away from overly polished “clean girl” dressing and back toward messier, more chaotic styling. Tiny denim shorts, layered belts, washed fabrics, revealing silhouettes and that whole “slightly trashy but glamorous” aesthetic are everywhere again this summer. A lot of what DSquared2 was doing in 2010 suddenly feels very connected to the current obsession with indie sleaze, Y2K paparazzi fashion and imperfect styling.
What once looked excessive now almost feels current again.
Collection Credits
Collection — DSquared2 Spring/Summer 2010 Ready-to-Wear
Designers — Dean Caten & Dan Caten
Brand — Dsquared2
Presentation — Milan Fashion Week Spring/Summer 2010
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 26, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people standing, wrist watch, cigarette and text that says 'The longer I watch, the more I get it. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
In “Dream your paintings, paint your dreams!” Tim Walker turns fashion into a surreal art installation inspired by the distorted figures and emotional intensity of Egon Schiele. The editorial feels less like a traditional fashion story and more like stepping inside a painting that’s constantly shifting between fantasy, performance and psychological theatre.
What makes Tim Walker’s work so recognizable is the way he approaches fashion photography almost like world building. The clothes are important, but atmosphere always comes first. In this story, twisted body language, exaggerated silhouettes and theatrical staging create images that feel strange, playful and slightly unsettling at the same time. Walker has always understood that the strongest editorials are the ones that completely transport you somewhere else instead of simply showing beautiful clothing.
The Schiele inspiration works especially well because the editorial never tries to imitate fine art too literally. Instead, it translates Expressionism into fashion imagery through movement, distortion and emotion. Kiki Willems and James Crewe almost look like living brushstrokes inside the set, while Jacob K’s styling pushes the silhouettes further into this surreal, off-balanced world. (fashionCOW￼)
What also makes the story stand out years later is how physical and handmade everything feels. In a time where so much fashion imagery becomes overly retouched or digitally polished, Tim Walker’s editorials still feel tactile, theatrical and deeply human. That’s probably why his work continues to feel timeless while so many campaigns disappear after one season.
Editorial Credits
Editorial — “Dream your paintings, paint your dreams!”
Publication — i-D Magazine Spring/Summer 2017
Photography — Tim Walker
Styling — Jacob K
Models — Kiki Willems, James Crewe
Hair — Shon
Make-up — Sam Bryant
Set Design — Emma Roach
Casting Director — Madeleine Østlie
Production — Jeffrey Delich
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 27, 2026. May be an image of miniskirt, shorts and text that says 'Dsquared SS2010 is what I wear this Summer. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
What makes this British Vogue story so powerful is the emotional weight behind every image. Editorials often try to imitate intimacy, but this one feels real. You can immediately sense the connection between Anok Yai and her mother, Nyibol Akuei, without the images needing to explain themselves. There’s a softness, protection and familiarity in the way they move around each other that makes the story feel much bigger than a normal fashion editorial.
Anok has spoken many times about how close she is to her mother, especially after her recent health struggles. In the Vogue interview, Nyibol describes how painful it was seeing her daughter in the hospital and how deeply connected they are as a family. That emotional closeness completely changes the energy of the editorial. It stops feeling like “fashion images” and starts feeling archival — almost like family history documented through luxury fashion photography. (British Vogue￼)
Visually, the editorial works because Rafael Pavarotti understands how to photograph Black skin with depth, richness and drama without flattening it into something overly polished. The lighting feels cinematic and spiritual at the same time. Every frame has scale, but the relationship between mother and daughter always remains the center of attention. That balance is incredibly difficult to achieve in fashion photography.
Anok Yai has always had one of the strongest presences in modern modeling because she understands narrative. She doesn’t just model clothes — she creates atmosphere. And here, that atmosphere feels deeply personal. The editorial quietly speaks about migration, family, survival, beauty and legacy without becoming performative or overexplained. That’s what separates a great editorial from a forgettable campaign: it leaves behind emotion, not just visuals.
Publication — British Vogue
Model — Anok Yai
Featuring — Nyibol Akuei
Photography — Rafael Pavarotti
Styling / Fashion Editor — Kate Phelan
Interview — Funmi Fetto
Creative Direction — British Vogue / Condé Nast creative team
Hair — Eugene Souleiman
Make-up — Hiromi Ueda
Set Design — Ibby Njoya
Production — Prodn Paris
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 27, 2026. May be an image of miniskirt, shorts and text that says 'Dsquared SS2010 is what I wear this Summer. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
This summer’s style mood feels less clean and much more personal. The best outfits right now look slightly chaotic, undone and thrown together in the best way, tiny shorts with oversized shirts, ballet flats with sportswear, messy layering, and vintage accessoires.
Sleaze and old runway styling, but styled in a more playful way. From Kate Moss-denim micro shorts and slouchy belts to colorful tights, fitted tanks, embellished shorts and oversized graphic tees, the focus is less about looking “perfect” and more about having character.
Even the more dressed-up looks still feel authentic. Smudged sunglasses, worn-in denim, oversized bags, ballet shoes, tiny gym shorts or random statement pieces make the outfits feel human instead of overstyled. This summer is really about mixing glamour with casualness, looking like you didn’t try too hard while still creating a fun silhouette and attitude.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 27, 2026. May be an image of miniskirt, shorts and text that says 'Dsquared SS2010 is what I wear this Summer. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
In Athletic Girl for Vogue Italia August 2016, Steven Klein turns fashion photography into a staged power fantasy. The editorial plays with dominance, sexuality and control, placing Lara Stone at the center of every image more like a character than a model. She doesn’t just wear the clothes, she completely controls the atmosphere of the story.
What makes the editorial so memorable is how fully it leans into that idea. The exaggerated poses, glossy styling and theatrical tension give the images a very specific energy that still feels striking years later. Lara Stone brings this raw, almost intimidating presence to the story, while Patti Wilson’s styling pushes everything further into this hyper-glamorous, slightly surreal world.
It’s the kind of editorial that understands fashion photography should create a mood, not just present clothes.
Editorial Credits
Editorial: Athletic Girl
Publication: Vogue Italia August 2016
Photography: Steven Klein
Styling: Patti Wilson
Model: Lara Stone
Male Model: Andres Sanjuan
Makeup: Diane Kendal
Hair: Shon
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 27, 2026. May be an image of miniskirt, shorts and text that says 'Dsquared SS2010 is what I wear this Summer. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
What makes the Zara x Bad Bunny collaboration feel so strange is the contradiction behind it. Over the past years, Benito built an image around political awareness, authenticity and cultural resistance. He positioned himself as an artist who speaks for marginalized communities and distances himself from the polished corporate machinery dominating both music and fashion.
That is exactly why this partnership feels so cynical.
Zara is not just another fashion brand. It is one of the largest fast fashion corporations in the world, built on mass production, aggressive consumption and the commercialization of identities. Watching an artist whose image is so closely tied to rebellion and anti establishment messaging suddenly become the face of that machine inevitably raises questions about how performative celebrity activism really is once global corporations get involved.
And the collaboration becomes even harder to defend creatively because the collection itself feels painfully uninspired. Oversized basics, washed tones, predictable silhouettes and a campaign aesthetic that looks indistinguishable from regular Zara menswear. There is nothing here that reflects the risk taking or visual boldness that made Bad Bunny culturally interesting in the first place.
Without Benito’s name attached to it, the collection would most likely disappear into the endless cycle of forgettable Zara drops within a week.
Of course, it would also be dishonest to pretend most celebrities would reject a collaboration like this. Fashion has always been tied to capitalism and almost every major artist eventually becomes absorbed into the same corporate structures they once appeared to criticize.
But with Bad Bunny, the contradiction feels especially glaring because so much of his appeal came from the idea that he was different from exactly this kind of system.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 27, 2026. May be an image of miniskirt, shorts and text that says 'Dsquared SS2010 is what I wear this Summer. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
At just 21, Raphael Badalov is making a name for himself in the global Chrome market – but how did he end up with a cache of Chrome Hearts pieces said to have belonged to Karl Lagerfeld, which he eventually sold for more than $1 million?⁠
⁠
On the On the site, we speak to the luxury reseller behind @byraph about tracking down the elusive archive, the mythology surrounding Lagerfeld’s Chrome obsession, and the one-of-one pieces now fuelling fashion’s archival gold rush.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to see more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @elliothoste⁠
1-2. Photography by Pascal Le Segretain via @gettyimages⁠
3-4. Courtesy of Byraph⁠
5. Photography by Andrew H. Walker via Getty Images⁠
6-7. Courtesy of Byraph⁠
⁠
#Dazed #KarlLagerfeld #ChromeHearts
posts.alt_text: Photo by Dazed on May 27, 2026. May be an image of football, soccer, sportswear, sports equipment and text that says '23 The Iraqi refugee designing football jerseys for all your favourite players DAZED'.

posts.caption:
As a child, Diana Al Shammari (@thefootballgal) was obsessed with the football anime series Captain Tsubasa and made a habit of stealing her brother’s jerseys. When she wasn’t watching the beautiful game, she was playing it, using football as a way to make friends and find a sense of normalcy. For the London-based embroiderer, football has always represented freedom.⁠
⁠
She first experimented with embroidery in 2017, after her sister came home with a football shirt in desperate need of some jazzing up. She embellished it with delicate floral stitching and soon began reworking vintage kits, transforming them into one-of-a-kind pieces.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to read more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @isobelvandyke⁠
📸 @aida_dahmani⁠
⁠
#DazedFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Dazed on May 27, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, magazine and text that says 'The mystery behind Karl Lagerfeld's $1m Chrome Hearts collection DAZED'.

posts.caption:
On RyanAir flights and high-speed trains to Berlin, people share tips on how dressing in all black and adhering to hidden vibe codes might secure access to the city’s industrial-sized nightclubs, where hedonism and trance-inducing techno beats run for days on end.⁠
⁠
These experiences have become synonymous with Berlin in the 21st century – in 2023, its techno scene was even added to UNESCO’s list of intangible cultural heritage sites. But, with the rise of parties like @toytonics in recent years, the beat of the city has started to shift to something more colourful.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to read more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @sp.mcc⁠
📸 @aliceestella⁠
⁠
#Dazed⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Dazed on May 27, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, magazine and text that says 'The mystery behind Karl Lagerfeld's $1m Chrome Hearts collection DAZED'.

posts.caption:
In 2023, women made up 80 per cent of the book-buying market in the UK, US and Canada. In the US, the gender gap in reading has persisted for years, with fiction-reading among men facing an especially precipitous decline (if there were any real takeaways from last year’s “performative male” obsession, it was that men who read now feel like such an anomaly that spotting one in the wild is cause for suspicion.) ⁠
⁠
With the reading gap between men and women continuing to widen, it’s not surprising that reading gap couples exist. But how much should these gaps actually matter in dating, and does a literary mismatch really have to be a dealbreaker?⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to read more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @isabelbekele⁠
📸 Still from The Drama, 2026⁠
⁠
#Dazed⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Dazed on May 27, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, magazine and text that says 'The mystery behind Karl Lagerfeld's $1m Chrome Hearts collection DAZED'.

posts.caption:
All your favourite fashion designers are finding inspiration inside a former horse hospital… ⁠
⁠
London’s @contemporarywardrobe has been documenting British youth culture for over 40 years, housing a collection of well over 20,000 pieces. ⁠
⁠
John Galliano, Phoebe Philo, Margiela and Burberry are all big fans, while musicians such as David Bowie, Harry Styles, FKA Twigs and Beabadoobee have all modelled the clothes. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption: Desi Bling was deeply, deeply unsettling to watch. I understand that reality television is heavily scripted and exaggerated for shock value, but this series pushed it into a territory so relentlessly misogynistic that I genuinely had to stop midway. Every time the husbands spoke, I felt like throwing something at the TV.

People wear gowns. People wear tradition.
People wear elegance. But then Aishwarya Rai walks in, and suddenly fashion stops being fabric and becomes a moment in history. Time slows down. Eyes stay.
Conversations fade. Because some women don't follow beauty they become its definition. Years will pass, Cannes will see countless stars, but some queens aren't remembered for a look; they become part of the legacy itself. And Aishwarya Rai remains that legacy. ♥️🙏🏼

Michael Preysman, the founder of Everlane, is launching a new fashion brand called ‘Still Radical’ in response to Everlane’s sale to Shein. While the product offering is unclear, the company is meant to bring back the “vision of Everlane but with a new take that meets the moment.”
Thus, the launch page for the as-yet-unnamed brand promises no private equity or venture capital involved in the business, which Preysman describes as “an experiment.” Perhaps then, if Everlane’s sustainability and transparency model represented the ‘millennial dream’ in the 2010s, Still Radical represents the same generation’s disenchantment with VC/PE; Where the dream used to be about organic fibers and responsible manufacturing, it is now about getting the capitalism out of capitalism.
The Shein sale, though, doesn’t inspire much confidence. Preysman, in a letter published by Vogue yesterday, claims that he didn’t know about the Shein sale until “20 minutes before,” and that the internet’s negative response to the sale comforted him as it demonstrated how consumers still crave sustainability and transparency. Over the weekend he “meditated” on the “ironic” Shein news before coming up with his radical new company idea.
“Today, the customer is looking for that hope again,” he writes. “The world has changed, and we’re in a period where capitalism is in full force. There isn’t quite the balance between capitalism and equity, and between capitalism and creativity.”
Silicon Valley startup fashion brands like Everlane haven’t been quick to learn the lessons that fashion industry brands have been learning for years: Fashion is volatile, unpredictable, moody. One day you’re in, and the next day you’re out! The business models that work for other consumer products don’t quite translate to fashion either. Hence, AllBirds, a Silicon Valley footwear brand, recently pivoted its business from shoes to AI.
Nevertheless, the tech world seems to view fashion as an extractable resource, one that comes with a measure of cultural clout which can’t be mined in other industries. Can they finally crack the code as a tech company that looks like a fashion brand? Or is it doomed from the start?

posts.caption:
Images of the White House lawn are going viral as construction for a UFC cage match on the site is underway (yes, really). The “UFC Freedom 250” event is slated for June 14, to coincide with the US’s 250th anniversary, Trump’s birthday and Flag Day.
“I’ve been involved in a lot of big events,” the president said about the fight last month. “I have never had an event that has had more interest than the UFC fight we have right at the front door.”
Trump first mentioned the idea last July 4 while talking about plans for the nation’s anniversary celebration. “Think of this on the grounds of the White House. We have a lot of land there,” he said. The statement came just one day after Vance was deployed to Pakistan for negotiations with officials in Tehran about six weeks into the war with Iran, per MS Now.
So to recap what’s happening at the White House complex: on one side, construction crews are building a ballroom where the East Wing once was. On the other, the Rose Garden has been paved over, replaced by a concrete patio. And now at the center, there’s a giant steel cage where fighters will punch each other in front of the president for his 80th birthday.
#idiocracy #news #reels #threads #washington

posts.caption:
We could go see Boop!
Quinta Brunson is set to develop and star as Betty Boop in a new film by her production company Fifth Chance Productions, alongside creator Max Fleischer’s grandson Mark Fleischer and Fleischer Studios.
According to the film’s press release, the story will trace Betty’s origin and evolution through Max Fleischer’s perspective, digging into the “relationship between the artist and his creation as he navigates the creative and commercial pressures of building one of the world’s first animated icons, particularly as that icon begins to take on a life of its own.”
Online reactions to Brunson stepping into the role are split. Some fans see the two-time Emmy winner as a perfect fit for the character’s reboot, while others are questioning her casting.
Betty Boop’s influence and origins have long-sparked debate. While Fleischer claimed to have based the character on a dog, singer Helen Kane famously sued the studio, arguing Boop was modeled after her. In court, the studio pointed the character’s iconic baby voice to Black performers of the 1920s, including Baby Esther, a popular child star.
Betty’s roots have been debatable for decades, but either way, she’s a fictional character and Brunson is a great choice for the legendary diva!
Swipe through for more of her fascinating history.
#bettyboop #quintabrunson #film #history #animation

posts.caption:
After years of soft neutrals, skin tints, and “no-makeup makeup” dominating the algorithm, Marc Jacobs Beauty is relaunching on its own terms with statement shades and party balloon-inspired packaging.
The brand first debuted in 2013 and quickly reached cult status before disappearing in 2021 after its partnership with Kendo ended. Now it returns under a new license with Coty Inc., re-entering a market that has fundamentally shifted.
While “clean girl” minimalism has dominated beauty since the brand shuttered, the cultural pendulum seems to be swinging towards color and maximalism, and away from former mega-brands like Glossier.
Marc Jacobs Beauty is returning with saturated pigments, high-gloss finishes, and sculptural packaging in a relaunch Jacobs calls a celebration of “creativity and self-expression.”
The rollout includes seven hero products priced from $26 to $42, reviving fan favorites like its best-selling gel liners in 21 shades, alongside new products like a multi-use blush stick.
Whether this return meaningfully disrupts the beauty category remains to be seen, but it’s clearly not trying to blend in.
Marc Jacobs Beauty drops May 28 on marcjacobs.com, followed by a June 1 release at Sephora.
#marcjacobs #beauty #makeup #marcjacobsbeauty #sephora

posts.caption:
On May 19th, Jonathan Andic was arrested in connection to the death of his father Isak Andic, the billionaire founder of Mango, who died in 2024 after slipping into a ravine on a Montserrat hiking trail. Authorities have outlined new evidence against Jonathan, prompting his arrest as a homicide suspect.
Jonathan, the only witness to his father’s death, claimed that he was a few meters ahead on the hiking trail when his father paused to take photos of the scenery. He told emergency services that he heard the fall, but did not see it occur. He also claimed that his relationship with his father was good, despite known tensions around Jonathan’s business failings at Mango in 2015.
However, new evidence shows that Isak’s cell phone was found in his pocket at the time of his death, and no photos appear on his camera roll of the hike. As well, the autopsy report found no scuffs on Isak’s hands, suggesting that he fell backward, not forward, into the ravine. Examination of Isak’s shoes also showed that the soles were worn down “deliberately” to create the appearance of a slip. Jonathan was also found to have visited the hiking trail multiple times shortly before his father’s death, causing investigators to suspect premeditation.
Most importantly, Jonathan’s tense relationship with his father was illuminated by Estefanía Knuth, the professional golfer and long-term girlfriend of Isak (who happens to be in a legal dispute with Isak’s children for €70m of the inheritance.) Knuth’s claims are supported by WhatsApp messages between Jonathan and Isak which reveal the son’s “obsession with money” and his demands for a living inheritance, which Isak was “forced” to agree to.
Finally, in 2024, Jonathan learned that Isak had planned to set up a “foundation to help people in need.” WhatsApp messages show a shift in the son’s attitude towards his father, causing him to express a desire to reconcile their differences. Thus, Jonathan invited Isak on the hike in Montserrat, just the two of them, to “talk alone.” Wanting to recover his relationship with his son, Isak accepted the invitation that would lead to his untimely death, and the foundation was never realized.

posts.caption:
A photo of the White House is going viral for capturing the absolute chaos happening on America’s front lawn right now. Very on-brand for this administration, honestly.
“All of this paid for by myself,” Trump shouted to reporters over a gaping pit and the sound of heavy machinery, insisting the renovations are a “gift” to the country.
According to the president, the new ballroom is also a new security flex for additional construction underneath it. “The ballroom becomes a shield for everything that you see here,” he said, describing it as “the one opportunity for the military to do something.” Guess the bunker theories were right all along.
The president’s comments come as Republicans struggle to pass nearly $1B in funding for ballroom security and other WH projects, despite Trump repeatedly insisting his passion project would not be paid for by taxpayers (shocking, right?)
Can Congress stop this Home Depot-gilded mess before he gets any more ideas, ffs?!
#idiocracy #viral #news #reels #threads

#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

#ELLECoverStar: Between takes, Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks about working with domestic violence and coercive control survivors, particularly within South Asian communities. About helping women reclaim confidence, voice, and agency within legal systems that often fail to recognise the complexity of their experiences.
“I haven’t spoken about this publicly before, but I spent ten years in an abusive, financially and coercively controlling marriage,” she shares. “It shaped how much of myself I could express. Reclaiming myself and healing has been a long process, and one of the first things I did was remove the name he gave me. I’m returning to my birthright, as Zinnia Jothi Rose.”
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

#ELLEFashion: Fashion’s latest trouser obsession comes with volume, movement, and a gathered hem. Balloon pants, once associated with early 2000s boho dressing, returned on the Spring/Summer 2026 runways with sharper styling, cleaner proportions, and a much cooler attitude. After years of denim dominating every conversation, fashion’s latest obsession wants movement, drama, shape, and a little chaos around the ankle. Fashion has spent the last few seasons flirting with volume. Bubble hems returned. Cocoon coats resurfaced. Skirts grew wider. Sleeves expanded. Balloon pants feel like the natural next chapter. They bring theatrical proportion without the stiffness of tailoring and carry the ease of loungewear without looking lazy. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read more

Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
From ’90s @viviennetam to Michael Kors-era @celine, IYKYK collections are the new status symbol.
Among a select portion of the fashion set, the allure of vintage dressing—in particular niche vintage dressing—has never been stronger. Wearing a brash designer logo or monogram used to be the ultimate status symbol. Then, simply wearing luxury brands conspicuously did the trick. Now, that’s been replaced by references that only your in-group would understand: The label is only half the flex; the era matters just as much.
“When a modern version of a brand is popping off, we’ll see a surge in the vintage version,” explains @janellelgray, co-founder of vintage rental platform @isleofmonday; after all, it's an opportunity to show off some fashion history know-how. “People want to pay homage to the designer back then and feel like they’re part of the conversation now.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Shakaila Forbes-Bell on October 14, 2025 tagging @secondhand_pigeon, and @fashionispsychology.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Shakaila Forbes-Bell on March 28, 2023 tagging @fumithelabel.

posts.caption:
I can not agree more with this.
This is exactly why I use the inner energetic map through Human Design as the core of my job, with fashion as a way to embody someone’s uniqueness rather than perform an identity.
For instance, with emotional beings, the process is not about “fixing” emotions, but learning to honor their emotional waves ( the ups and downs ) through awareness, self-observation, and even personal fashion choices. Clothing becomes a language for the body, a way to support and express what is truly being felt.
Guiding people to reconnect with their body wisdom and energetic mechanics instead of getting trapped in the rabbit hole of the mind…
Fashion then stops being performance or conditioning… and becomes a process of feeling, embodying, and truly being. 💖
Curious about your thoughts about this 🙏🏻✨

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
In our dreams, *every* celebrity is wearing secondhand and or more sustainable brands ✨⁠
⁠
But in reality? The biggest brands with the biggest budgets and the biggest problems get all the attention 😵‍💫 To help out, we have a few suggestions for more sustainable brands that trending public figures should consider 👀⁠
⁠
For @keke, why not something playful from @jannjune? For @sombr, why not a classic with a twist from @seekcollective? For @annehathaway, why not a timeless item from @ayaniclothing? For @pascalispunk, why not innovative menswear from @theglade.au? For @sabrinacarpenter, why not a frilly fun look from @rupahaus? The options are endless 💚⁠
⁠
We’ll see if these celebrities will take our advice but, for now, save this post in case you’re looking for some inspiration while keeping sustainability in mind 💫 Visit the link to explore our Directory, where you can discover more ethical brands rated “Good” and “Great” ⬆️⁠
⁠
#CelebrityStyle #Fashion #CelebrityFashion #CelebrityStylist #OOTD
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Whatever you do…SKIP TEMU 😭⁠
⁠
The ultra fast retailer is one of our worst rated, representing what a lack of transparency and action looks like, resulting in abused labour, excess waste, and the questionable treatment of animals.⁠
⁠
Did we mention they’re helping to enable overconsumption and overproduction? They’re doing that too—and our experts suggest doing whatever you can to *not* shop there 🤢⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn why Temu rates so poorly 👀 What are your thoughts on Temu? Visit the link for the full rating along with more sustainable alternatives ↑⁠
⁠
#Temu #TemuFinds #TemuHaul #TemuShopping #Sustainability⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
No one wants to wear plastic. And yet: so many of us are 😢⁠
⁠
From a report we conducted last year, echoed by the 2024 Circularity Gap Report, the vast majority of materials being made are synthetics—which means most clothes being made come from synthetics 😵⁠
⁠
Synthetics do not biodegrade and shed microplastics when washed. They are an environmental disaster, all of which is propelled by being fast fashion’s material of choice. What you wear reflects your values and plastic is likely *not* the vibe you’re trying to exude 🚮 Visit the link to learn more about this issue and about better alternatives ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #OOTD #ZaraStyle #HMStyle #SHEINGals
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on May 22, 2026. May be an image of plastic bag, garbage and text that says 'Are you wearing plastic clothes? Most likely Synthetic materials like polyester account for 69% of all fibres produced, as we discovered in a report conducted in 2025. This may sound excessive but is echoed by the 2024 Circularity Gap Report by Circle Economy, where it was found synthetic fabrics account for approximately 60% of what's being made. This is why it's highly likely your clothes are plastic. Visit the link to learn more about better materials and the dangers of plastic clothes'.

posts.caption:
Naomi Osaka continues to serve up a Grand Slam tourdrobe worthy of celebration - not criticism.
Yes, we admire her 120 mph serve. Of course we’re obsessed with the backhand of this powerhouse mother, currently ranked 15th in the world, not to mention her lobs and volleys. But can we please take a minute to deep dive her court-side couture too?
Critics said the sweeping black number by Kevin Germanier, which she peeled off to reveal a shimmering bodysuit designed to resemble the Eiffel Tower, was distracting. We say the golden sequinned tennis ensemble (courtesy of bespoke Nike) for the French Open was up there with her other fashion moments, like the aquatic jellyfish number in Melbourne, by upcoming couturier Robert Wun in collab with Nike. We love her unapologetic self expression as she turns the court into a catwalk on the Grand Slam tour.
Serving aces AND looks. What do you make of the way she plays it?

Want to get your glow on? TAP the link in bio to shop the best next-gen bronzers.

posts.caption:
One of the advantages of playing Kate Moss? Digging into her wardrobe archive.
Ellie Bamber wore the supermodel’s 1997 Calvin Klein look for the premiere of her new movie Moss & Freud. It tells the story of Kate Moss sitting naked for a portrait by acclaimed artist Lucien Freud in the early noughties. The film features many of Kate Moss’s iconic looks from the era, but it now looks like the red-carpet rundown will also include a few fashion easter eggs. We’re here for them.

posts.caption:
Fashion has no shortage of beautiful faces, but @alexconsani represents a new generation of supermodels—the ones not just walking the runway but creating platforms, building communities, and leading with personality. First gaining attention on TikTok, Consani is now one of the world’s most sought after models. “I’m having fun,” says Consani. “I’m doing it for all 50 of the other bitches that would love to be in that room.” For Bazaar’s Summer 2026 issue, the supermodel talks about trying her hand at acting, moving into her big-girl home, and entering her “experimental era.”
-

posts.caption: Inside this week’s issue of HELLO!, we take you behind the scenes of Prince William’s emotional week following his extraordinarily candid appearance on Heart Breakfast. ❤️ We also dive deep into the unique bond between Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor and Sarah Ferguson’s relationship 30 years after their divorce. Plus, don’t miss our exclusive coverage of MobLand star Lisa Dwan’s stunning wedding to Paul Henninger, featuring a surprise, star-studded guest list including Geri Halliwell and Bill Nighy. Finally, Ioan Gruffudd’s wife, Bianca Wallace, discusses their high-profile blended family and the incredible way pregnancy offered a respite from her MS battle. 🫶

posts.caption:
@kith and @leomessi link up with @adidasfootball to mark two milestones at once: Kith’s 15th anniversary and Messi’s 20 years since his international debut. ⁠
⁠
The result is a collection that sits right between performance and lifestyle, pairing on-pitch essentials with everyday tailored and cut-and-sew pieces.⁠
⁠
Across footwear, goalkeeper jerseys, suits, denim jackets, tracksuits, and more, the drop moves seamlessly between match-day function and off-duty ease. It’s a wardrobe built for both sides of the game, where stadium-ready gear and casual staples share the same language, anchored by Messi’s legacy, Kith’s design lens, and adidas' know-how and resources. The collection is slated to be released on May 29 via Kith.

posts.caption:
It's giving Cher.⁠
⁠
@alysaxliu brought full rockstar energy to the 2026 American Music Awards, stepping onto the red carpet in a custom all-black @chromeheartsofficial look layered with standout jewelry. The Olympic gold medalist stacked heavy silver chains, rings, and statement accessories across the fit, pushing the dark, punk-inspired styling even further with a silver bridge piercing and matching metallic manicure details designed to resemble facial jewelry.⁠
⁠
Presenting the award for Best Rock/Alternative Artist, Liu fully embraced the rising wave of edgy Gen Z beauty trends. Whether the bridge piercing is permanent or not, the Chrome-heavy styling and rebellious details cemented the look as one of the night’s boldest fashion moments.

@diesel and @tinder’s Pride 2026 collection reimagines Diesel’s signature “For Successful Living” motto with “For Successful Loving.” The drop is a 17-piece capsule that is all about amplifying LGBTQIA+ freedom. Will you be picking up something from the line?

posts.alt_text:
For the past decade, boat shoes have been associated with playgrounds, school uniforms and adolescence. Teenagers turned the original sailing essential into their own school staple, but fashion is co-opting this trend. From @miumiu to @aritzia, boat shoes are taking over, and we’re here for it. Are you hopping on this one?

Images: Courtesy of Brands, Getty Images

posts.caption:

British eyewear brand @cutlerandgross teamed up with punk icon Debbie Harry to drop a trio of sunglasses, celebrating her artistry and attitude.
The Blondie frontwoman designed a lineup including retro-inspired cat eye silhouettes, complete with a gold-foil “DH” logo as a final personal touch.
You can now shop the collection online and in Cutler and Gross stores.

posts.caption:
It’s no surprise that proud Canadian @champagnepapi is dressing Team Canada World Cup with an off-field @nocta kit. ⁠⚽️🇨🇦⁠
⁠
While the rapper is still riding the wave from his 3-part album drop with Iceman, Habibti and Maid of Honour, his @nike born label is readying for the 2026 World Cup alongside the Canadian National Men’s Team @CANMNT.⁠
⁠
NOCTA is officially joining Nike’s X2 collaboration lineup for the 2026 FIFA World Cup, joining recently teased collabs with  G-Dragon, Slawn, Jacquemus, and more. Front and center is the NOCTA Nike Cryoshot: a reimagining of the Tiempo 94  with a gold and black makeover, characterized by a fold-over tongue embossed with the NOCTA North Star logo.⁠ 🌟⁠
⁠
A tribute to Canadian heritage, the pre-match collection fuses NOCTA’s street grit with national identity, utilizing the country's maple-leaf crest across tracksuits and jerseys rendered in bold black, red, green, and yellow hues. ⁠
⁠
The NOCTA x CANMNT X2 Collection is expected to drop on SNKRS, Nike, and select retailers as the World Cup approaches.⁠
⁠
Photos: NOCTA / Nike⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 28, 2026 tagging @hypetrak, @dontoliver, @hbx, and @hypebeastmag. May be an image of magazine, poster and text.

posts.caption:
@liluzivert is rolling out a new album — on his pet monkey’s Instagram page, @piccola.piccola.piccola16 🐒
Yes, you’re reading that right.
Piccola, Uzi’s pet monkey, now has a new Instagram account, which seems to be where the rapper is beginning to roll out a new album. Thus far, Piccola has shared a photo dump (captioned “Early Studio sesh”) and a video with Uzi himself.
Tap in with Piccola now👀
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 28, 2026 tagging @hypetrak, @dontoliver, @hbx, and @hypebeastmag. May be an image of magazine, poster and text.

posts.caption:
Two iconic backpacks in one 🎒⁠
⁠
@beams_america is already a favorite of patchwork pieces like the Harris Tweed Balmacaan Coat and its Madras shirting; now it’s bringing its collage-like approach to @jansport and @eastpak’s iconic backpacks. In celebration of the Japanese brand’s 50th anniversary, the “Hybrid Pack” collaboration combines signature elements from both brands into a single functional and collectible piece. ⁠
⁠
The JanSport x Eastpak “Hybrid Pack” is out now, exclusively at BEAMS America.⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by HYPEBEAST on April 28, 2026 tagging @hypetrak, @dontoliver, @hbx, and @hypebeastmag. May be an image of magazine, poster and text.

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
@umathurman Chooses Happiness: For decades, Thurman has embodied a certain kind of unstoppable presence, and  this month, the icon returns to the grisly genre that cemented her stardom, as a bloodthirsty avenger in #PrettyLethal. But off-screen things are less grim: Thurman is energized by her work and looking forward to the possibilities of her next chapter—one where she’s making space for new roles, new interests, and more time for herself: “I think the choice to be happy is one that should be made regardless of any conditions.”
Read more now at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio.
InStyle March 2026

posts.caption:
Kritika Kamra (@kkamra) and Gaurav Kapur (@gauravkaps) have been household names for years. He’s the OG VJ-turned-sports anchor and host of Breakfast with Champions; she went from primetime TV to silver screens and streaming favourites. Lately, though, the internet can’t stop talking about their marriage.
“The first thing anybody says to me now is, ‘How’s married life?’ And I want to say, it’s all the same. Except now I live with my best friend, and I don’t have to decide what’s for lunch every day.” says Kamra.
For their first-ever cover shoot post marriage, Kritika and Gaurav take Lifestyle Asia India on a little getaway with The Honeymooners.
Editor: Analita Seth (@analitaseth)
Photographer: Sarang Gupta (@saranggupta)
Fashion Editor: Neelangana Vasudeva (@neelangana)
Produced by Pranjal Sharma (@praanjallll)

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

«Ci sono persone davvero brave in determinate cose, e tutte insieme funzionano in modo brillante, ma non tutti possono essere eccellenti in tutto. Negli anni ho imparato a capire cosa ti motiva e in che modo puoi essere utile a un progetto, soprattutto quando lavori in collaborazione», dichiara @KateHudson nella sua intervista con L’OFFICIEL.
«Se metti insieme persone di cui ti fidi e che sono brave in quello che fanno, ottieni la migliore esperienza possibile. Quando un ambiente di lavoro è instabile o caotico, raramente porta al successo».

@ChanelOfficial ha presentato la sfilata Métiers d’art 2026 di @Matthieu_Blazy a Seoul, negli spazi del nuovo Centre Pompidou Hanwha, che aprirà ufficialmente le sue porte al pubblico il 4 giugno 2026 nel quartiere finanziario di Yeouido, all’interno dell’iconico complesso 63 Building.

posts.caption:
C’era una volta YSL. Prima che la moda diventasse un sistema, era una visione. Yves Saint Laurent l’ha avuta più in anticipo di chiunque altro. Il #LeSmoking nel 1966 — quando nessuna donna indossava uno smoking. Il vestito Mondrian nel 1965 — quando l’arte e il guardaroba erano ancora due mondi separati. Il safari jacket, il tuxedo femminile, il body come fondamenta di tutto. Un archivio che non è archivio: è il presente che non sa ancora di esserlo.
Photo: Getty Images
#YvesSaintLaurent #HauteCouture #LOfficiel #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠
